Project Description:
Rise Again is a seven-story residential building that explores a missing middle housing approach, with a focus on community, rest, and connection. The number seven is intentional. It draws inspiration from the idea of rest, particularly the concept in Christianity where the seventh day is a day of rest. This idea is translated into architecture as a building that supports both daily life and moments of pause.

At the ground level, the first floor is designed as a shared community space.
It includes a community hall and kitchen, which act as the social heart of the building and allow residents to gather, share meals, and build relationships.

Bike parking is also located on this level, promoting sustainable transportation and supporting everyday convenience.

The upper six floors are dedicated to residential units. Each floor contains eight units, creating a consistent and efficient layout.

The repeated mountain motif across the building represents the mountains that the building itself partially blocks.

The façade becomes a symbolic gesture, reflecting the presence of the mountains and maintaining a visual connection to the landscape.

This idea is reinforced at the rooftop level, where residents have access to shared outdoor space with direct views of the mountains. This creates a full experience, from symbolic representation on the façade to real physical connection at the top.

The building is situated between two existing structures, which can create a sense of density and enclosure. To respond to this condition, I chose a palette of white and yellow.

White helps reflect light and makes the building feel more open and less heavy within the tight urban context. 

Yellow introduces warmth and brightness, helping to bring light into the space and create a more uplifting and welcoming atmosphere.

Together, these colours soften the density of the surroundings and enhance the overall experience of the building.

Project Description: Our project counters Cadillac Fariview's proposal of the Unilever Precinct site. Instead of massive office towers adjacent to single family housing neighbourhoods, we instead propose moderate density which is contextually sensitive. We reject CF's proposal for white- collar office jobs and instead harken back to the site's industrial past by promoting blue-collar work with new trade school. The old industrial quiality is celebrated in its raw state with minimal yet intentional interventions. The trade school acts as an anchor for the new East Harbour Transit Hub attracting workers and visitors from all over Toronto and Beyond.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Row Housing, Infill Housing, Agriculture and Gardening
Project Description:
This village explores housing as a framework for community, centered on an inward-facing mews that fosters everyday social interaction. Rather than treating circulation as a purely functional element, the design repositions access as a social spine, where front doors, elevated walkways, and shared outdoor spaces collectively encourage informal encounters and cultivate belonging.

The site strategy is anchored by the retention of an existing mature tree, preserving a natural gathering point and organizes the public realm. From this anchor, the proposal establishes a deliberate gradient of public, semi-public, and private spaces, that supports both collective activity and individual retreat. The central mews serves as the primary organizing element, framed by residential units whose primary entrances face inward to enhance visibility, safety, and community engagement.

The building massing responds sensitively to context and program. A curved residential form defines the site's interior while lifting at strategic moments to introduce natural light, permeability, and vertical connections. Exterior walkways and terraces extend circulation into shared space, enabling residents to engage with the mews beyond their unit thresholds.

A diverse mix of unit types (studios, one, two, three-bedroom units) is distributed throughout the building to accommodate varied household needs. Integrated refugee housing is dispersed across multiple levels and locations to promote inclusion within the broader village rather than isolation. Proximity to shared amenities reinforces accessibility and social integration.

Precedents such as Peter Barber Architects' Edgewood Mews and Barcelona's Born Market inform the spatial and landscape strategies, particularly the use of simple forms, topography, and pedestrian-focused sequences to support flexible social use. Ultimately, Village Mews is a resilient system for fostering connection, community resilience, and shared urban life.

Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Prefabrication, Shared Spaces, Co-Housing, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
The Interstice reimagines a site currently occupied by a church, proposing a redevelopment that better serves both the congregation and the surrounding community. The project introduces shared spaces designed to support community use, in collaboration with the nearby Immigrant Services Society of BC (ISS of BC).

As one of three residential buildings within a broader master plan, two of which are developed by Kyon Gicole, The Interstice focuses on providing housing for those seeking refuge. The unit mix prioritizes two- and three-bedroom layouts to accommodate families and larger households.

The building employs a single-loaded corridor strategy, enabling cross ventilation for passive cooling within the units. This exterior corridor also creates a generous overhang, offering shade during the summer while allowing solar access in the winter. Beyond its environmental performance, the corridor functions as a shared social space, encouraging interaction and fostering a sense of community among residents.

Outdoor areas are shaped by the building footprints, responding directly to site conditions and user needs. A tiered master plan minimizes shadowing between structures while maintaining adequate space for housing and essential services.

The Interstice demonstrates how thoughtful redevelopment can give back to the community, creating opportunities for connection, support, and shared public life through the spaces it defines.

Project Description:
Designed in collaboration with Alessandro Di Bari, the site seeks to reinterpret the spatial qualities of the traditional European city square such as enclosure, walkability, and social interaction, then translating these principles into the contemporary urban fabric of Vancouver. The southeastern building includes a community kitchen and rooftop balcony, providing shared spaces to support the congregation, residents, and the broader local community.

Project Title: Thresholds of Resilience: Sheltering the Vulnerable, Without Isolation
Image Description/Alt Text\n: A rendering of the courtyard
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Option 9, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
How do we house people who have lost their homes without cutting them off from the life of a community? This question sits at the heart of Thresholds of Resilience. Responding to the "ReZoned" challenge, the project transforms underused church land into a place where below-market housing and refugee housing are not "added on" but woven in.

The masterplan starts by strengthening the existing neighborhood. A carefully renovated church is joined by a new residential block. By removing private parking, the site becomes a pedestrian-first "Shared Ground" - not a plaza in name only, but a daily gathering space with a community kitchen where church members, ISSofBC staff, and all residents can cook, eat, and linger together. This is not an event space; it is an everyday place. At street level, this thick wall opens into an inhabited colonnade - a covered walkway where neighbors can pause, meet, or simply sit without feeling exposed to the street's noise. 

The courtyard designed for calm, not spectacle. Gentle water features mask city sounds. A canopy of pink cherry blossoms - chosen to honor the magnolia's quiet symbolism of resilience and home-offers shade and beauty. The courtyard is shared by all residents, safety here comes from mutual presence, not surveillance.

This thinking continues inside. Single-loaded corridors are facing the courtyard, act like elevated green walkway. These are places to nod hello, to see children playing below, to feel part of something. A simple structural grid allows for diverse 1-to-4-bedroom homes, with refugee housing distributed across all floors, indistinguishable from market units. Equity is not announced; it is built. Instead of exposed balconies, winter gardens are carved into the façade, private, sheltered, but still connected to the life of the building.

In the end, this project is about housing as a foundation for everyday community where people can finally rest and once again, be resilient and hopeful.

Project Description:
This project is part of the larger Cascade Residence development, organized as a linear housing bar that frames a continuous shared outdoor space. The design transforms a typical residential strip into a social spine, where circulation, landscape, and community life intersect.

Ground-level pathways and green spaces encourage interaction and movement, while the articulated massing breaks down the scale and creates rhythm along the length of the building. Units are arranged to maximize light, views, and connection to the shared space.

The project explores how linear living can support a more connected and community-driven residential experience.

A central break in the massing suggests a key പ്രവേശ or shared circulation point, reinforcing the idea of a connected spine that links the development together.

A central feature—likely a plaza or focal installation—anchors the composition, while softer landscaping elements such as trees and shrubs create a more intimate, human-scaled environment. The layout balances movement and pause, encouraging both circulation and social interaction within a semi-enclosed green spine.

Project Description:
The Stack is designed as a spark for social reintegration for the
unhoused population of New Westminster. By prioritizing intentional
spatial adjacencies, the project facilitates a transition from recovery to
stability. The program is divided into two residential tiers: individuals
currently undergoing rehabilitation for homelessness and substance
use, and those who have successfully transitioned through similar
supportive frameworks. These two demographics converge within
the central tower—a dedicated social volume designed to foster
peer mentorship, mutual support, and a shared sense of community
through lived experience. The Stack punctures into the library below
and is the driving force for spatial organizations, intended for people
to gather at the center and create connections of their own.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Coach House is a counter-proposal to the current development at the former Toronto Coach Terminal. Informed by GIS-based site studies of demographics, green coverage, and tree canopy, the proposal reimagines what a tower-and-podium typology can mean in downtown Toronto. Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Community land trusts, Housing design, Housing theory, Pedagogy, Prefabrication, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Towers, Mixed Use
Project Description:
The Coach House is a counter-proposal to the Kilmer-Tricon redevelopment of the former Toronto Coach Terminal at Bay and Elizabeth.
The site has been cleared before. In 1858 it was a dense residential neighbourhood; today, residential coverage has fallen to 5%, a 90% loss from peak. Tree canopy sits below 10%, green space under 5%, and the Bay Street Corridor holds just 24% of Toronto's average density of children. This is not where families live.
The project refuses that pattern. Grounded in GIS analysis of demographics, canopy, circulation, and family infrastructure, it argues that memory and affordability are not opposed to density; they are its precondition. Where Kilmer-Tricon proposes 873 studios and one-bedrooms above a paramedic hub and office podium, The Coach House proposes 394 family units above a full K-8 elementary school for 542 students and a licensed daycare for 83 children. Elizabeth Street is closed to traffic and reopened as a pedestrian boulevard linking both buildings to a new park.
Two volumes work within the site's heritage designation and 55-metre flight-path limit: a 15-storey building on Bay Street with the school across floors 1 to 4 and housing above, and a 43-storey tower on Elizabeth with daycare at grade, shared amenity on floors 3 and 4, and family units to floor 43. Units rotate 45 degrees off the building axis, a diamond geometry that gives every home dual aspect, cross ventilation, and two balconies. Sizes range from 70 m² to 145 m², with 60%+ affordable tenure.
The result: 45% fewer units than the existing proposal, but more residents, six times more school-age children, and an active public ground. The heritage terminal becomes a community event space. School and daycare anchor the base. Families live above. Public land, finally, returns public benefit.

Home Studio\n: Manufacturing Otherwise — University of Manitoba
Tags: Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Infill Housing, Mixed Use, Land Back, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
It begins with a fragment of birch bark, often left behind. As a material-led design, it rethinks how materials are sourced, how waste can be revalued, and how architecture can participate in both ecological and social systems. Embedded within and expressed through the walls, birch bark cork becomes a tactile architectural material rooted in care, memory, and cultural practice, while challenging conventional material chains. 

Situated in the Spence Neighbourhood of Winnipeg, Manitoba, and developed in partnership with the Spence Neighbourhood Association (SNA), the project is designed for those who already inhabit and rely on the neighbourhood, particularly the local Indigenous community. It supports individuals who arrive during the day seeking resources, and to those who stay, offering transitional housing. The project exists within a condition of tension between innovation and feasibility, care and constraint, and between what is possible and what is permitted. For SNA, the urgency is practical, where spaces must welcome and endure. For architecture, the responsibility is broader, to move beyond extractive practices and toward accountability and care. 

The design is rooted in an intention to place Indigenous values at the centre of architectural making, and to position design as a medium for restoring connection and overcoming alienation. Birchstead Living operates as a negotiation between systems and stories, structure and softness, efficiency and care. Here, living is held not only within walls. It proposes an architecture where material, people, and place are continuous and never left behind. Re-grounding architecture as a practice of care and connection that holds memory while making space for what comes next.

Project Description:
This project combines a public library with supportive women’s housing to create a place of safety, learning, and belonging. The proposal responds to the need for stable housing that supports women and families experiencing housing insecurity while also providing access to community resources, education, and social connection.

The building is organized with a public library at the lower levels and residential housing above, maintaining clear separation between public and private spaces. Housing is arranged around a protected internal courtyard with shared gardens, play areas, and informal gathering spaces. Units transition through semi-private thresholds such as balconies, pocket spaces, and exterior corridors that support autonomy and choice.

Guided by trauma-informed design principles, the project emphasizes safety, clear sightlines, layered privacy, and welcoming communal spaces. Prospect and refuge inform both library and housing areas, balancing openness with shelter. Natural materials, daylight, ventilation, and adaptable screens create a warm, non-institutional environment that supports healing, dignity, and long-term stability.

Project Title: Reproducing the Suburb: Housing, Childcare Infrastructure, and the Gendered Burdens of Caregiving in Toronto’s Densifying Peripheries
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Black-and-white photograph of a suburban backyard in 1943 Canada, showing a mother saying goodbye to her two young children at a small outdoor daycare area with swings, as a caregiver stands nearby, illustrating wartime childcare in a suburban context.
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Housing design, Housing theory, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Multi-Plex Housing
Project Description:
Foregrounding the contemporary housing crisis as co-constitutive of the gendered and spatial crisis of caregiving, this paper advances a feminist critique of suburban housing and planning policies in Toronto. It centers Scarborough—one of Canada’s most underserved childcare districts—as a critical site of analysis, where the shortage of childcare infrastructure is compounded by ongoing suburban densification. This transformation is driven by mid-rise development and the proliferation of additional dwelling units under the City’s Expanding Housing Options in Neighborhoods (EHON) initiative, which currently proceeds without an adequate framework for the corresponding expansion of childcare capacity. In response, the project calls for the systematic integration of childcare infrastructure into urban policy and new housing, positioning it as essential social infrastructure that must be coordinated with housing planning, policy, and design.

Moving beyond its consideration within affordability, labor markets, and social policy, the project examines childcare as a problem structured through architecture and urban planning. It highlights the disconnect between contemporary densification policies and the region’s childcare infrastructure shortfall, while foregrounding how caregivers—primarily women, and especially women of color in low-income neighborhoods—negotiate the urban environment and perform invisible labor to sustain social reproduction. Through this analysis, the paper proposes amendments to EHON through the co-location of childcare infrastructure within new mid-rise housing along transit-rich Major Streets.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: The revitalization of Vanauley Walk is illustrated through the activation of the ground plane, where 50% of existing townhouses are adapted into commercial and live/work spaces. New triplexes, fourplexes, and sixplexes are introduced to increase density while maintaining the neighbourhood’s scale.
Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Prefabrication, Shared Spaces, Infill Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
Alexandra Park is a multicultural, lower-income neighbourhood in downtown Toronto shaped by repeated cycles of erasure and renewal. Originally developed as public housing following 1950s “slum clearance,” and again transformed in the late 2000s, the site reflects a tabula rasa approach to revitalization. Despite this, a strong and diverse community emerged, formalized through the 2003 conversion to the Atkinson Housing Co-operative—the first of its kind in Canada—granting residents collective control over their housing. 

Today, this community faces renewed pressure. Recent proposals by Tridel prioritize market-rate units over rent-geared-to-income (RGI) housing, threatening displacement. In response, SOCA proposed an alternate vision that retains the existing townhouses while relocating some residents to new condos.

Our proposal aims to retain as many townhouses and existing residents as possible in order to preserve the neighbourhood’s small-scale, socially cohesive character, while increasing density to accommodate Alexandra Park’s growing population. The main pedestrian-oriented pathway (Vanauley Walk) is central to the site; to activate it and improve safety, 50% of the existing ground-floor townhouses are converted into commercial or live/work spaces, allowing residents to operate businesses below their homes. Given the small unit sizes, two ground-floor units can be combined to create larger commercial spaces for uses such as a pharmacy, or reconfigured into accessible units. Newly proposed triplexes, fourplexes, and sixplexes help accommodate displaced residents within similar housing typologies. Although a larger community center is proposed along Grange Ave, smaller scale community spaces such as a shared kitchen, lounge and dedicated youth and senior drop-in areas are integrated along Vanauley Walk to extend programmatic access and support everyday social interaction at a more intimate, neighbourhood scale.

Project Title: The Ontario Science Centre: A Call for Evolution
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Intervention on the Ontario Science Centre Building B - Proposed Observatory/Look out.
Student Name(s): Nava Mahouri Monfared, Carolina Ramirez Tenorio
Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Pedagogy, Agriculture and Gardening, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
A Call for Evolution stands as a manifesto against the privatization of the Ontario Science Centre. We advocate for a programming evolution that honors Raymond Moriyama’s original vision: a public cultural asset rooted in the nature of the site.
We firmly reject proposals for office-heavy privatization of the building. As a non-negotiable community anchor, the Science Centre is becoming one of Toronto’s most accessible nodes via the new Ontario Line and Eglinton LRT. Privatizing this landmark would strip the community of a vital resource just as it becomes most reachable. Our proposal follows Moriyama’s own directive that the building’s program be reassessed every 15 years, pivoting the OSC toward ecology and hands-on sustainability.
While the original design framed the Don Valley, it lacked a mechanism for visitors to physically engage with the ravine. Our design expands the architecture through branching stilted pathways and satellite buildings. This is informed by Moriyama’s childhood experience building treehouse while in a Japanese Canadian internment camp, an act of finding solace in nature that defined his lifelong architectural philosophy.
By using stilted substructures, we respect the delicate wetlands and watershed while allowing direct immersion into the Woodlands, Open Lands, and Meadows. Through new spaces for urban agriculture and outdoor classrooms, we transform the site into a living laboratory. This proposal aimes to ensure the Science Centre remains a public, accessible, and essential educational heart for all people.

Image 8 Alt/Description: The Open Land satellite is more of an observatory watchtower, reflecting the flatter topography. It extends views directly to the Don River. The curriculum here focuses on the Staghorn Sumac, Creeping Thistle, Red-tailed Hawk, Great Mullein, and Cattails."

We’ve mapped exactly where it is appropriate to build and where we must stay sensitive to the watershed. We’ve identified three distinct zones: the Meadows, Open Lands, and Woodlands. Each has unique flora, fauna, and soil conditions.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
“Brighter Waters” is a counterproposal for Port Credit that challenges prevailing models of heritage conservation and suburban growth. In contrast to the adjacent Brightwater development, critiqued for its siloed approach to activity, connectivity, and densification, the project proposes a more integrated urbanism that is active, continuous, and capable of evolving. Working across urban and architectural scales, it introduces new east–west connections that link the Heritage Conservation District to the town center, waterfront, and key transit nodes.

The proposal focuses on the Old Port Credit Village Heritage Conservation District, understood not only as a protected enclave but also as a spatial and regulatory barrier to broader connectivity. Rather than treating heritage as fixed, the project advances a model of targeted, incremental redevelopment that enables adaptation while maintaining material and cultural significance. Conservation is reframed as an active, ongoing process.

At the architectural scale, the project studies a representative property containing original brick from the historic Port Credit Brick Company, embedding local industrial history within its construction. The house is reconsidered as part of a larger assemblage with an adjacent property, forming a combined site with greater capacity for intensification, programmatic diversity, and spatial reorganization.

The proposal explores regenerative reuse through additions, mixed-use programming, shared space, and selective preservation. Mature trees are treated as organizing structures, extending designation beyond the building to the landscape. The house is activated through new uses and connections, allowing it to participate in neighbourhood life.

By aligning material continuity, incremental growth, and public connectivity, “Brighter Waters” strengthens relationships between past, present, and future urban conditions.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Angled mixed-use library and housing building at the corner of Front Street Mews and McKenzie Street, viewed from above, featuring a stepped podium courtyard with outdoor seating, landscaping, and exterior stairs connecting street level to a shared terrace, with residential levels above organized around the open courtyard.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Courtyard Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
This project is a mixed-use civic and residential building located in downtown New Westminster, at the intersection of Columbia Street, Front Street Mews, and McKenzie Street. The proposal combines a public library with affordable housing to create a single, integrated building that supports both community life and long-term living.

The library forms the civic base of the project, acting as an accessible public resource that goes beyond traditional programming. It provides spaces for learning, gathering, and support services such as job assistance, language resources, and community outreach. The goal is to position the library as everyday infrastructure that serves a wide range of users, including newcomers and the broader public.

Above, the residential component follows a mixed housing model, combining non-market housing with long-term stabilization housing for newcomers. This approach supports both permanent residents and individuals transitioning into stable housing. Units are designed to be flexible, adaptable, and supportive of different household needs, prioritizing dignity, privacy, and long-term livability while avoiding institutional qualities.

A key idea in the project is the blending of public and residential users through shared spaces that encourage interaction while still maintaining clear boundaries. These spaces create opportunities for connection without forcing it, allowing residents and library users to coexist comfortably.

The building responds to its urban context by addressing the slope of McKenzie Street, engaging the more public Columbia Street edge, and activating the quieter Front Street Mews. The overall design aims to feel cohesive as one building, reinforcing the idea that housing and public life are interconnected.

The drawing illustrates spatial organization, including a shared courtyard level connecting library and housing, stepped massing responding to site slope, and active ground interfaces on Columbia Street and Front Street Mews. Environmental systems are integrated throughout, including a green roof for rainwater collection, solar panels, natural ventilation, high-performance envelope, and a geothermal system below grade.

Tags: Housing design, Prefabrication, Housing Production, Towers, Mixed Use, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
A second, miniaturized version of Canada's Earth Tower, theorized and purposed by Perkins & Will with the same goals and vision: To be the livable, high-performance, mass timber residential tower of the future. 

The project achieves Passive House Classic levels of performance, while challenging the typical "vancouverist" window wall clad high-rise common around the lower mainland. 

This was done by breaking the monolithic tower form into three separate masses. Each mass is 7 to 8 storeys tall, a height similar to the 6-storey mid-rise buildings popping up all over the region. Which in my opinion, strikes a good balance between density, livability and socialability.

The horizontal breaks on the tower are shared outdoor amenity spaces, which allows residents access to the outdoors without having to go all the way to grade or up to the roof.

The library itself has carried this theme of the future into its planning and design. The library revolves two ASRS systems (like those used in IKB at UBC) which can retrieve books or items from storage racks for easy distribution and storage. Thus freeing up all the space that would've been taken up by stacks for more flexible/modern programming like maker spaces and study/group work areas.

grad show boards anna...

This project is designed to combine the two social groups that are most affected by loneliness and help them build a community based on mutual support, while being affordably housed. the design approach for the housing cluster is inspired by a slavic traditional log house entry vestibule which acts both as a social hub and a thermal buffer. main project goals were to provide opportunities for gradual connection, to maximize passive ventilation, and explore straw-bale panels as a thermal barrier in application with prefabricated timber construction in order to decrease carbon emissions from the construction process.

Project Title: Reclaiming the  Commons:We Demand an Integrated Regent Park

Project Description: Phases 4 and 5 of Toronto’s Regent Park renewal plan include amenities such as community centers and outdoor green spaces. However, a persistent lack of interaction remains between Toronto Community Housing (TCHC) tenants and market-rate residents in the larger Regent Park, Canada’s first public housing development. This thesis promotes an integrated Regent Park by embedding common spaces in a new mid-rise TCHC building and public courtyard, fostering daily interaction. Reclaiming the Commons redistributes communal space through community kitchens, prayer and meditation rooms, double-height reading spaces, family and senior lounges, and a collective rooftop garden, etc. creating a spatial gradient from public to semi-private to shared communal life.

Project Title: From Isolation to Connection: Reimagining Senior Housing as Collective Commons
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Sectional perspective showing senior-friendly circulation from residential buildings through a central atrium to the mall, with accessible paths and landscaped courtyards connecting buildings above ground.
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Courtyard Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description: This project explores how seniors’ social housing can move beyond isolated living to support everyday connection by retrofitting Toronto’s East York Town Centre and transforming a space built for consumption into one that supports care and community. Rather than separating housing and retail, the proposal reimagines the mall as shared social infrastructure, integrating daily services, informal encounters, and accessible public life. The design introduces shared programs, clear and inclusive circulation, and spaces that encourage interaction while still allowing privacy, balancing independence with collective living. An indoor connection between the residences and the mall enables safe movement during winter, while light-filled openings improve spatial quality in lower levels. Responding to the Right to Housing, the project strengthens habitability, accessibility, and a sense of belonging, proposing housing not only as shelter but as a framework for social life, intergenerational exchange, and mutual support.

Project Description: This project is designed to bring nature into everyday life by connecting the public realm, shared courtyard spaces, and upper-level gardens into one continuous green system. The design supports outdoor living by creating spaces for gathering, resting, walking, gardening, and enjoying fresh air as part of daily routines. Project Title: Tower-with-its-Park: Re-envisioning St. James Town’s Housing Towers and Their Park Lands
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Rendering of a courtyard
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Financialization, Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Towers, Mixed Use
Project Description: Tower-with-its-Park reimagines Toronto’s St James Town’s postwar tower-in-the-park housing as community-centered, dignified homes. Once designed for the working class, the privately-owned towers now house lower-income immigrant communities in overcrowded, neglected conditions typical for the aging, inequitable rental stock. This thesis demands for sustained reinvestment in Toronto’s housing towers to ensure habitable living conditions, support community life and cultural exchange. Deploying a speculative public-private retrofit funding model, this project upgrades infrastructure, integrates environmental strategies, and reconfigures units for diverse households with flexible partitions and expanded balcony enclosures, while reclaiming shared grounds through vibrant ground-floor communal programs and diverse green space.

Project Title: KENSINGTON MARKET SUPPORTIVE HOUSING: A Stable Anchor for Formerly Unhoused Residents

Project Description: Many unhoused residents in Toronto are repeatedly displaced across shelters, encampments, and informal urban spaces, without stable access to services. This project focuses on 35 Bellevue Avenue in Kensington Market, a publicly owned site within one of the city’s most service-dense areas. It proposes approximately 105 units of supportive, affordable housing, operated through partnerships between the City, KMCLT, and St. Clare’s, integrating housing, services, and community programs. We demand stable, service-connected urban housing where formerly unhoused residents can remain, access support, and anchor themselves within the city. The project reframes homelessness as a spatial failure, positioning architecture as urban support infrastructure that enables stability, reduces displacement, and supports long-term living.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: An oblique site plan and perspective section of the repurposed abattoir.
Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Cooperative housing, Financialization, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Mixed Use, Agriculture and Gardening, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
In the 1960, the City mistook the Civic Abattoir as a temporary financial liability for a permanent burden. By selling to Quality Meat Packers, the abattoir that was built to prevent monopolization was traded for a one-time payout, ultimately surrendering the once public land to exclusive private development. The counterproposal begins in 1960 — the City retains the site of the abattoir rather than surrendering it to private ownership. Along with the adjacent Wellington Destructor and city-owned land, the abattoir becomes an opportunity for community-focused public development. 

The buried Garrison Creek will be retraced conceptually. 2 Tecumseth Street will transition from industrial slaughter to a city-owned living commons of affordability, creativity, and resilience.

In the era of deindustrialization and reintroduction of the creek we propose: 
Affordable housing, including co-ops and RGI units,
Creative workshops and artist markets to replace industries, 
Resilient communities supported by the Creek and public amenities. 

The main abattoir building is repurposed for a municipal-run grocery store, community kitchens,  and creative studio spaces. The facade is modified to reflect the program change and the skylight is extended over the proposed interior street.

Project Title: Rooted Reuse: Reclaiming Industrial Heritage for Collective Artist Housing and Production
Image Description/Alt Text\n: An interior perspective sketch of a flexible circulation space doubling as a shared space.
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Community land trusts, Cooperative housing, Financialization, Housing design, Housing theory, Housing Production, Participatory Design, Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description: Adaptive reuse of light industrial buildings can address Toronto’s housing crisis while preserving cultural heritage. This thesis explores how reclaiming these disused structures can support live/work artist communities and advance the right to housing through affordability, security of tenure, and accessibility. The project calls for the transfer of underused industrial building stock into the stewardship of a cultural land trust as a city-wide network of permanently affordable artist housing and workspaces. Rooted Reuse treats existing structures as durable shells, inserting maker spaces within and layering housing above to form a dense, multi-level neighbourhood ecosystem that sustains livelihoods and cultural continuity. Developed through a prototype proposal at 40 Wabash Avenue, the project tests a replicable model for broader city-wide implementation.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Left: Site plan at 1:500 scale depicting the site and scale of the project. Right: The ground floor plan that houses an auditorium, backstage areas, administrative rooms, and multiple makerspaces.
Image 1 Caption: The project anchors itself within an existing industrial footprint, retaining the industrial shell as a spatial and cultural datum. At grade, the building operates as a public-facing plinth: a large auditorium, administrative functions, and distributed makerspaces establish a porous civic interface that supports both production and gathering.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Second and third floor plans showing a dense arrangement of makerspaces organized around circulation corridors and a central commons. Insets depict a shared workshop with long tables and tools, and a flexible exhibition space with wall-mounted artwork and open floor area.
Image 2 Caption: Upper plinth levels intensify productive use through a range of makerspace scales organized around shared work commons. Flexible exhibition areas are embedded within circulation, allowing production and display to coexist, reinforcing the building as an active cultural infrastructure rather than a static container.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Fourth and fifth floor plans introducing residential units arranged around a central corridor, with labeled residential units, accessible washrooms, and communal spaces. Insets show a performing arts studio with open floor space and a multipurpose room configured for group work.
Image 3 Caption: The transition from production to dwelling begins with hybrid live/make floors, where residential units are interwoven with shared creative spaces. This layering supports a gradient of activity while maintaining proximity between domestic and productive life.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Sixth and seventh floor plans showing residential layouts and shared corridors. Insets include a barrier-free bedroom, a residential kitchen, and a long workspace overlooking the industrial interior.
Image 4 Caption: Upper levels prioritize collective living through various housing typologies and adaptable domestic arrangements. The inclusion of age-in-place rooms and barrier-free design ensures long-term residency, while visual connections to the makerspaces below maintain continuity between living and making.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Composite interior perspective showing a timber-framed circulation corridor with plants and seating on one side, and a glass-enclosed workshop with tools and workbenches on the other, set within an existing brick industrial structure.
Image 5 Caption: A perspectival sketch reveals the core spatial strategy: lightweight timber volumes inserted within a heavy industrial shell. Circulation becomes a social spine, mediating between enclosed maker spaces and the retained brick structure, framing production as a visible and collective activity.
Image 6 Alt/Description: A series of diagrams including phased structural drawings, program distribution diagrams using color coding, module axonometrics, and logic diagrams illustrating the transformation of the existing building into layered live-work volumes.
Image 6 Caption: Analytical diagrams articulate the project’s logic: the retention of the industrial shell, the insertion of modular timber units, and the vertical stratification of program. Together, they describe a scalable system for adaptive reuse that supports a networked model of decommodified live/work housing.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Mixed Use
Project Description: This building is a two-storey library with six storeys of 60 affordable housing units above. The project aims to create intentional community connection through architectural expression. The housing fosters an environment where residents can get to know their neighbours, building stronger and more resilient communities. This is achieved through exterior circulation and generous front door “porches.” Each level features a shared laundry and games room, encouraging neighbours to mingle. At level 3, housing amenity spaces blend with the library, creating visual connections to the library below and further promoting community interaction. Within the library, an atrium is cut out along the slope of the site to connect Columbia and Front Street. This creates an inviting space for passersby, where community events could take place.
